reviews.summary: This paper presents ParaDG, a training-free inference method that attempts to improve text-to-image diffusion model robustness to prompt rewording by blending paraphrases gated by their disagreement variance. The authors conduct an exploratory study and report a negative result: the disagreement gate does not outperform simpler ungated paraphrase blending. The paper is well-positioned as an honest negative-result study with transparent reporting of limitations.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I conducted 5+ online searches to verify novelty: (1) Exact title search found no prior ParaDG papers. (2) Core technique search (disagreement gate + paraphrase blending) found related but distinct work. (3) AAPB (Lee et al. 2026) is the closest related method - it also blends auxiliary prompts adaptively but uses a closed-form coefficient based on Tweedie's identity rather than variance-based gating. (4) MetaLogic (Shen et al. 2026) and SSA (Feng et al. 2023) evaluate paraphrase robustness but don't propose inference-time methods. (5) Multi-prompt blending has been studied but the specific combination with variance-based disagreement gating appears novel, though the components are not. The paper acknowledges AAPB as the closest baseline but was unable to reproduce it faithfully. The novelty is incremental - the method combines existing ideas (multi-prompt blending, variance-based weighting) in a plausible but ultimately ineffective way.
strengths: Honest presentation of negative results - the paper clearly states that the disagreement gate does not improve over ungated blending, which is valuable scientific knowledge
Transparent acknowledgment of limitations - Table 1 explicitly lists which planned experiments were not completed (AAPB reproduction, human validation, etc.)
Well-structured experimental design with multiple baselines (vanilla SD1.5, static consensus, adaptive ungated) and ablations
Confidence intervals reported for all metrics, enabling proper statistical interpretation
Paraphrase audit with controlled templates and Cohen's κ=1.0 for consistency checking
Complete experiment workspace with code, logs, and results.json properly documented
Official T2I-CompBench-family metrics used for evaluation, ensuring comparability
weaknesses: The pilot showed completely flat results across all 9 gate/schedule combinations (all tied at 0.2083), suggesting the core mechanism may be fundamentally ineffective rather than needing tuning
Small evaluation scale - only 60 prompts for main faithfulness evaluation, 24 for robustness
Very low relations scores across all methods (0.06-0.18) suggest the benchmark may be too difficult or the metric too strict for meaningful discrimination
Missing key baselines - faithful AAPB reproduction and strong single-prompt baselines (Attend-and-Excite, Self-Coherence Guidance) were planned but not completed
No human evaluation - the paper relies entirely on automatic metrics which may not capture perceptual quality trade-offs
Slot-aware redistribution is only evaluated in combination with the gate, making it hard to isolate its contribution
The gate magnitude traces show very small values (~10^-3), suggesting the variance signal is too weak to be useful
reviews.detailed_feedback: The authors should focus on strengthening the comparison with AAPB and other single-prompt guidance methods. Since AAPB is now accepted to CVPR 2026 and uses a similar auxiliary-prompt blending approach, a faithful reproduction is essential for positioning. The authors should also consider: (1) analyzing why the variance signal is so weak and whether normalization or a different disagreement statistic could help, (2) testing on a larger prompt set to improve statistical power, (3) adding human evaluation even on a small subset to validate the automatic metrics, (4) isolating the slot-redistribution component in ablations, and (5) considering whether the paraphrase set (only 2 per prompt) is sufficient for meaningful variance estimation. The paper is well-written and the negative result is honestly reported, but the incomplete baseline comparisons limit the strength of the conclusions.
questions_for_authors: The pilot showed identical scores across all 9 configurations. Did you investigate why the gate and schedule had no effect, and does this suggest the variance-based approach is fundamentally unsuited to this problem?
The gate traces show magnitudes of ~10^-3. Is this sufficient to meaningfully affect generation, and have you tried alternative normalization strategies?
Why were the AAPB reproduction and strong single-prompt baselines not completed, and can these be prioritized for a revision?
Have you considered whether 2 paraphrases provide enough signal for variance estimation, and would more paraphrases help?
The relations scores are very low (<0.19) across all methods. Is this a limitation of the evaluation metric or do all methods truly fail on spatial relations?
reviews.integrity_check: Results integrity check PASSED. I verified that: (1) Experiment source code exists and implements the described method (exp/paradg/run.py, exp/shared/runner.py, exp/shared/generation.py), (2) Logs show actual training runs with reasonable values (exp/paradg/logs/events.jsonl, exp/vanilla_sd15/logs/events.jsonl), (3) Numbers in the paper's tables exactly match results.json - e.g., Table 1 ParaDG attribute binding 0.4677 [0.3629, 0.5723] matches JSON mean 0.4677 with CI [0.3629, 0.5723], (4) All cited references are real and verified via online search - T2I-CompBench (Huang et al. 2023, NeurIPS), T2I-CompBench++ (Huang et al. 2025, IEEE TPAMI), Attend-and-Excite (Chefer et al. 2023, ACM TOG), AAPB (Lee et al. 2026, arXiv/CVPR), MetaLogic (Shen et al. 2026, ICFEM), (5) Code implements variance-based gating as described in the paper (generation.py lines 189-193).

reviews.summary: The paper studies a training-free text-to-image inference method, ParaDG, that blends denoising predictions from audited prompt paraphrases and scales the correction by cross-paraphrase disagreement. Across 60 held-out prompts and a 24-prompt robustness subset on Stable Diffusion v1.5, the reported outcome is negative: ParaDG is competitive with simple baselines on category metrics but does not outperform adaptive ungated paraphrase blending on robustness.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I performed the required online novelty checks: exact-title searches for the submitted paper returned no prior publication, but exact-title searches for the cited closest work confirmed real nearby papers including Adaptive Auxiliary Prompt Blending (AAPB), MetaLogic, Structured Semantic Alignment, Attend-and-Excite, MaskDiffusion, Self-Coherence Guidance, and NeuroPrompts. The closest prior art is clearly AAPB, which already studies training-free multi-prompt blending for target-faithful diffusion generation; MetaLogic and SSA already frame logically equivalent / perturbed prompts as a robustness lens. I did not find an exact prior paper combining audited paraphrase-only auxiliaries with a disagreement gate and slot-aware redistribution, so the method is not copied verbatim, but the contribution is a narrow specialization of an existing multi-prompt guidance family rather than a genuinely new direction. Given that strong overlap, I view novelty as limited and incremental rather than substantial.
strengths: The paper is unusually honest about negative results and clearly narrows its claim to what was actually supported.

The implementation and artifact structure are relatively strong: source code exists for generation, evaluation, aggregation, and dataset preparation, and the main reported table values match the stored result summaries.

The comparison against paraphrase-based controls is scientifically well targeted for the stated hypothesis: vanilla SD1.5, static consensus, and adaptive ungated blending isolate whether disagreement gating itself adds value.
The paper reports uncertainty intervals in the main tables and the conclusions appropriately note that most apparent gains are within overlapping confidence intervals.

Results integrity appears reasonably good: figures have machine-readable source files, generation indices contain row-level metrics and output paths, and logs show real execution of ideation, experiment reruns, official benchmark backfilling, and paper compilation.
weaknesses: Novelty is modest because the closest conceptual space is already occupied by AAPB-style multi-prompt blending; the main difference is a narrower paraphrase-restricted gating variant, which feels incremental.

The strongest planned baselines were not completed: faithful AAPB reproduction, the single-prompt baseline, and human validation components are all missing, which materially weakens the paper's significance and rigor.
The robustness story remains exploratory because the key robustness metrics are automatic and the preregistered human reliability / double-annotation components were not executed.

The paraphrase audit is still largely automatic and template-driven; although the artifact is better than 'accepted by construction,' it is not convincing evidence that semantic equivalence is reliably preserved in a human sense.
The study is tightly scoped to a narrow slot schema and SD1.5, so even if the negative result is correct, its broader impact on modern text-to-image systems is uncertain.

Reference positioning is mostly real and verifiable, but some metadata for very recent works appears fragile or lightly asserted, and the empirical section does not actually compare against the closest external family it emphasizes in related work.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper would be substantially stronger if it were reframed as a careful workshop-style negative result or artifact-backed exploratory study rather than a conference-ready method paper. To improve it, the highest-priority step is to complete at least one faithful closest-family comparison, ideally AAPB or a similarly strong recent multi-prompt guidance baseline, under the same SD1.5 setup. Second, add human validation for a stratified robustness subset, since the central claim is about semantic invariance under equivalent prompt rewrites and the current PRS pipeline is still automatic. Third, tighten the novelty claim further: position ParaDG explicitly as an incremental ablation-driven study of whether disagreement gating improves over ungated paraphrase consensus, not as a broad new guidance method. Finally, clarify the audit protocol and limitations of the automatic secondary review, and discuss more directly why gate magnitudes are tiny and whether that reflects a flawed statistic, weak scaling, or an inherent limitation of paraphrase disagreement as a signal.
questions_for_authors: Can you provide a faithful AAPB or comparable recent multi-prompt baseline under the same sampler, seeds, and evaluation protocol?
Why are the gate magnitudes so small in the released traces, and did you test stronger rescalings beyond the reported pilot without violating the pilot protocol?
How sensitive are the conclusions to the automatic paraphrase audit, especially for numeracy existential rewrites and relation prompts?
Do human raters agree that adaptive ungated blending is actually more robust than ParaDG on a sampled subset, or could the automatic PRS ranking be misleading?
How much of the negative result is specific to SD1.5 and the narrow two-object slot schema versus likely to transfer to newer diffusion backbones?
reviews.integrity_check: I did not find evidence of fabrication. The workspace contains substantial experiment source code, per-experiment result files, row-level generation indices, figure source JSON/CSV files, and logs showing real execution and official metric backfilling. The main table values in the paper are consistent with the stored summaries, and the released gate-trace and plot source files are coherent with the reported narrative. The main integrity concern is incompleteness rather than falsification: several preregistered baselines and human-validation components were skipped, so the conclusions should remain exploratory.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes ParaDG, a training-free method for Stable Diffusion v1.5 that blends audited paraphrases of a prompt during denoising, gated by the variance (disagreement) among paraphrase-conditioned predictions. The main result is explicitly negative: the disagreement gate does not outperform simpler ungated paraphrase blending on faithfulness or robustness metrics across 60 held-out prompts and a 24-prompt robustness subset. The paper is transparent about this finding and about the fact that 4 of 8 planned experiment components were revised to infeasible.
reviews.novelty_assessment: Online searches confirmed that no prior work proposes the exact combination of restricting multi-prompt blending to audited paraphrases with a variance-based disagreement gate. The closest work is AAPB (Lee et al., CVPR 2026, arXiv:2603.19158), which performs adaptive auxiliary prompt blending using Tweedie's identity. ParaDG is positioned as a paraphrase-restricted specialization of this family. However, the novelty is modest: the paper's own negative result demonstrates that the novel component (the gate) is the unhelpful ingredient, while the helpful ingredient (ungated paraphrase consensus) is not itself new. No substantially similar prior work was found via searches on Google Scholar, Semantic Scholar, and arXiv for 'paraphrase blending diffusion,' 'prompt ensemble diffusion,' 'multi-prompt blending text-to-image,' and related queries.
strengths: Exceptional transparency: the paper explicitly labels itself as an exploratory negative-result study and documents every missing component (AAPB reproduction, single-prompt baseline, human validation) in a completeness table (Table 1) rather than hiding omissions.
Clean experimental design with a well-structured prompt matrix (72 prompts, 144 accepted paraphrases, controlled rewrite templates) and formal paraphrase audit with slot-level preservation checks.

All numbers in the paper's tables match the stored results.json files precisely, and the experiment source code (exp/shared/generation.py) faithfully implements the described method including the gate, slot redistribution, and consensus blending.
95% confidence intervals are reported in all main tables, making the statistical weakness of the result immediately visible to the reader.
The writing is clear, well-organized, and the hypothesis-testing framing (Section 3.4) makes the scientific question precise and falsifiable.
weaknesses: Half the planned experiments were marked 'revised infeasible' (AAPB reproduction, single-prompt baseline, human reliability subset, human double annotation), leaving the study without its most important external baseline and without human validation. The paper cannot credibly claim to have tested the hypothesis against the relevant alternative methods.

The negative result is somewhat unsurprising given the diagnostic evidence: all 9 pilot configurations tied at exactly 0.2083 mean category score, and gate magnitudes were on the order of 1e-3. These signals suggest the gate mechanism was essentially inert from the start, which could have been detected and addressed before the full study.

Only 3 seeds were used, and no formal statistical significance tests (e.g., paired permutation tests, Wilcoxon) are reported beyond overlapping CIs. The CI overlap argument, while reasonable, is informal.

The study is limited to a single backbone (SD1.5) and a narrow slot schema (at most two objects). No experiments on SDXL, SD3, or other architectures limit the generalizability of the negative conclusion.

The robustness subset is small (24 prompts), and the ablation subset is even smaller (18 prompts for matched ablations, 12 for non-equivalent comparison), providing limited statistical power for the secondary claims.
The Cohen's kappa of 1.0 for the paraphrase audit is from an automatic-vs-automatic check, not human annotation, making the quality assurance on semantic equivalence weak.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper's greatest strength—its honesty—is also what reveals its incompleteness. As it stands, the study tests a narrow hypothesis (does disagreement gating help over ungated blending?) but lacks the most informative comparison: a faithful reproduction of AAPB or another strong single-prompt baseline like Attend-and-Excite. Without these, the reader cannot assess whether any form of multi-prompt blending is competitive with established training-free guidance methods, which significantly limits the significance of the negative finding. To strengthen this paper for resubmission: (1) Complete the AAPB reproduction and include at least one strong single-prompt baseline (Attend-and-Excite is publicly available). (2) Investigate why the gate magnitudes are so small—if the variance statistic is fundamentally too weak, consider alternative disagreement measures (e.g., cosine disagreement, attention-map divergence, or CLIPScore divergence across paraphrases). (3) Run experiments on at least one additional backbone (SDXL or SD3) to test whether the negative result is architecture-specific. (4) Add formal statistical tests. (5) Complete at least a small-scale human validation (even 20 prompts would help). The paper is well-written enough that these additions would make it a solid negative-result contribution.
questions_for_authors: Why were all 9 pilot configurations exactly tied at 0.2083 mean category score? Does this indicate a ceiling/floor effect in the pilot evaluation, or a bug in the pilot metric computation?
Have you investigated alternative disagreement statistics beyond L1 variance (e.g., cosine disagreement across paraphrase predictions, or attention-map divergence)?
The gate magnitudes are on the order of 1e-3. Did you experiment with removing the min(1, ...) clamp and increasing gate_c substantially (e.g., c=100 or c=1000) to force a stronger correction?
Why was the AAPB reproduction marked infeasible? The paper is on arXiv and the method description appears self-contained. Was it a code-availability issue or a resource constraint?
Would the negative result change on a model with a different text encoder (e.g., SDXL with OpenCLIP, or SD3 with T5)? The prompt sensitivity problem may manifest differently across architectures.
reviews.integrity_check: Results integrity is satisfactory. All numbers in Tables 3-6 of the paper match the corresponding values in exp/paradg/results.json, exp/vanilla_sd15/results.json, exp/adaptive_ungated/results.json, exp/static_consensus/results.json, and the ablation results files. The experiment source code in exp/shared/generation.py implements the described method correctly (disagreement gate at line 190-191, slot redistribution at lines 194-197, consensus blending at line 199). Experiment configurations in results.json files confirm the right flags (use_gate, use_slot_redistribution) for each method. 26 Python source files are present across the exp/ directory. Figures have corresponding source data files (JSON and CSV). No evidence of fabrication or inconsistency was found.
